title: Number of coral bleaching events by stage of the ENSO cycle
subtitle: [Coral bleaching](#dod:coral_bleaching) typically occurs when water temperatures rise above the normal range for the coral's habitat.  This is more likely during El Niño stages of the [ENSO cycle](#dod:enso_cycle) when tropical sea temperatures are warmer.
caption:
Number of coral bleaching events by stage of the ENSO cycle

Coral bleaching typically occurs when water temperatures rise above the normal range for the coral's habitat.  This is more likely during El Niño stages of the ENSO cycle when tropical sea temperatures are warmer.

The bar chart displays the annual count of coral bleaching events from 1980 to 2016, categorized by the stage of the ENSO cycle: El Niño (maroon), Moderate (purple), and La Niña (blue). The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 80 events. In the early 1980s, counts are generally low, with El Niño years showing approximately 20 to 25 events around 1982 and 1987, while Moderate years remain below 10. A significant spike occurs in 1998 during an El Niño phase, where the number of events reaches approximately 73, the highest value on the chart. Following this peak, the period from 2001 to 2006 shows a cluster of Moderate years with values ranging between roughly 18 and 40 events. A distinct peak for La Niña appears in 2010, reaching approximately 53 events, which is higher than most El Niño years shown aside from 1998 and 2016. The final years display a return to El Niño dominance, with 2015 recording around 30 events and 2016 reaching approximately 54. Throughout the timeline, Moderate years frequently register between 5 and 20 events, while La Niña years typically stay below 15, except for the 2010 outlier. The data illustrates variability in event frequency across different climate phases over the 36-year period.
